instruction: How do I implement proper async error handling in Express.js/TypeScript that catches both synchronous and asynchronous errors without try-catch in every handler?
output:
<think>
Express.js has a known limitation: it doesn't catch errors from async handlers automatically. If an async handler throws or a promise rejects, Express won't catch it — the process gets an unhandled rejection. The solutions: 1) Wrap every async handler with a helper that catches rejections and forwards to `next(err)`, 2) Use express-async-errors package (monkey-patches Express), 3) Build a custom wrapper. The cleanest TypeScript approach is a higher-order function `asyncHandler` that wraps async route handlers. For error handling middleware, Express recognizes error handlers by their 4-parameter signature `(err, req, res, next)`. We should create: a custom `AppError` class hierarchy, the async wrapper, and centralized error middleware. Important TypeScript considerations: proper typing for Request/Response, generic error handler that distinguishes operational errors from programmer errors, and proper HTTP status code mapping. Express 5 will natively handle async errors, but Express 4 needs the wrapper. I should also show how to handle errors from middleware chains and how `next(err)` skips to the error handler.
</think>

Here's a comprehensive async error handling system for Express/TypeScript:

```typescript
import express, { Request, Response, NextFunction, RequestHandler } from 'express';

// === Custom error hierarchy ===
export class AppError extends Error {
  constructor(
    public readonly statusCode: number,
    public readonly code: string,
    message: string,
    public readonly details?: string[],
    public readonly isOperational = true
  ) {
    super(message);
    this.name = 'AppError';
    Error.captureStackTrace(this, this.constructor);
  }
}

export class NotFoundError extends AppError {
  constructor(resource: string) {
    super(404, 'NOT_FOUND', `${resource} not found`);
  }
}

export class ValidationError extends AppError {
  constructor(details: string[]) {
    super(422, 'VALIDATION_ERROR', 'Validation failed', details);
  }
}

export class UnauthorizedError extends AppError {
  constructor(message = 'Authentication required') {
    super(401, 'UNAUTHORIZED', message);
  }
}

export class ConflictError extends AppError {
  constructor(message: string) {
    super(409, 'CONFLICT', message);
  }
}

// === Async handler wrapper (eliminates try-catch boilerplate) ===
type AsyncHandler = (
  req: Request,
  res: Response,
  next: NextFunction
) => Promise<any>;

const asyncHandler = (fn: AsyncHandler): RequestHandler => {
  return (req, res, next) => {
    Promise.resolve(fn(req, res, next)).catch(next);
  };
};

// === Central error handling middleware ===
const errorHandler = (
  err: Error,
  req: Request,
  res: Response,
  next: NextFunction
): void => {
  // Log all errors
  console.error(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] ${req.method} ${req.path}`, {
    error: err.message,
    stack: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? err.stack : undefined,
  });

  if (err instanceof AppError) {
    // Operational error: safe to send details to client
    res.status(err.statusCode).json({
      error: {
        code: err.code,
        message: err.message,
        ...(err.details && { details: err.details }),
      },
    });
    return;
  }

  // Programmer error: don't leak internals
  res.status(500).json({
    error: {
      code: 'INTERNAL_ERROR',
      message: 'An unexpected error occurred',
    },
  });
};

// === Usage: clean handlers without try-catch ===
interface UserService {
  findById(id: string): Promise<{ id: string; name: string } | null>;
  create(data: { name: string; email: string }): Promise<{ id: string }>;
}

const userService: UserService = {
  async findById(id) {
    if (id === '999') return null;
    return { id, name: 'Alice' };
  },
  async create(data) {
    if (data.email === 'taken@example.com') {
      throw new ConflictError('Email already registered');
    }
    return { id: '1' };
  },
};

const app = express();
app.use(express.json());

// No try-catch needed — errors propagate to errorHandler
app.get('/users/:id', asyncHandler(async (req, res) => {
  const user = await userService.findById(req.params.id);
  if (!user) throw new NotFoundError('User');
  res.json(user);
}));

app.post('/users', asyncHandler(async (req, res) => {
  const { name, email } = req.body;
  if (!name || !email) {
    throw new ValidationError(['name and email are required']);
  }
  const user = await userService.create({ name, email });
  res.status(201).json(user);
}));

// Must be registered AFTER all routes
app.use(errorHandler);

app.listen(3000, () => console.log('Server running on :3000'));
```

instruction: How do I build a validation middleware system for Express/TypeScript using Zod schemas with automatic request parsing and type-safe handlers?
output:
<think>
Combining Zod with Express gives us runtime validation plus compile-time type safety. The pattern: define Zod schemas for body/params/query, create a middleware that validates against them, and infer TypeScript types from the schemas so handlers get properly typed request objects. Key design: 1) A generic `validate` middleware factory that takes schemas for different parts of the request, 2) Use `z.infer<typeof schema>` to derive types, 3) Replace `req.body`/`req.params`/`req.query` with validated+transformed data, 4) Return Zod's detailed error messages in a consistent format. The middleware should handle Zod's `safeParse` which returns either success with parsed data or failure with structured errors. For the type-safe handler, we can extend Express's Request type with a generic that carries the validated types. This gives handlers full autocomplete and type checking. I should also show schema composition (reusing schemas), transforms (coercing strings to numbers for query params), and custom error formatting from ZodError.
</think>

Here's a type-safe validation middleware system with Zod:

```typescript
import express, { Request, Response, NextFunction, RequestHandler } from 'express';
import { z, ZodSchema, ZodError } from 'zod';

// === Validation middleware factory ===
interface ValidationSchemas {
  body?: ZodSchema;
  params?: ZodSchema;
  query?: ZodSchema;
}

interface ValidatedRequest<
  TBody = any,
  TParams = any,
  TQuery = any
> extends Request {
  validatedBody: TBody;
  validatedParams: TParams;
  validatedQuery: TQuery;
}

function formatZodErrors(error: ZodError): Array<{ field: string; message: string }> {
  return error.errors.map((e) => ({
    field: e.path.join('.'),
    message: e.message,
  }));
}

function validate(schemas: ValidationSchemas): RequestHandler {
  return (req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction): void => {
    const errors: Array<{ location: string; field: string; message: string }> = [];

    if (schemas.body) {
      const result = schemas.body.safeParse(req.body);
      if (result.success) {
        (req as any).validatedBody = result.data;
      } else {
        errors.push(
          ...formatZodErrors(result.error).map((e) => ({ ...e, location: 'body' }))
        );
      }
    }

    if (schemas.params) {
      const result = schemas.params.safeParse(req.params);
      if (result.success) {
        (req as any).validatedParams = result.data;
      } else {
        errors.push(
          ...formatZodErrors(result.error).map((e) => ({ ...e, location: 'params' }))
        );
      }
    }

    if (schemas.query) {
      const result = schemas.query.safeParse(req.query);
      if (result.success) {
        (req as any).validatedQuery = result.data;
      } else {
        errors.push(
          ...formatZodErrors(result.error).map((e) => ({ ...e, location: 'query' }))
        );
      }
    }

    if (errors.length > 0) {
      res.status(422).json({
        error: {
          code: 'VALIDATION_ERROR',
          message: 'Request validation failed',
          errors,
        },
      });
      return;
    }

    next();
  };
}

// === Schema definitions ===
const createUserSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(2).max(100),
  email: z.string().email(),
  role: z.enum(['user', 'admin', 'moderator']).default('user'),
  age: z.number().int().min(13).max(150).optional(),
  tags: z.array(z.string().min(1)).max(10).default([]),
});

const userIdParams = z.object({
  id: z.string().uuid('Invalid user ID format'),
});

const paginationQuery = z.object({
  page: z.coerce.number().int().min(1).default(1),
  limit: z.coerce.number().int().min(1).max(100).default(20),
  sort: z.enum(['name', 'created_at', 'email']).default('created_at'),
  order: z.enum(['asc', 'desc']).default('desc'),
});

const updateUserSchema = createUserSchema.partial().refine(
  (data) => Object.keys(data).length > 0,
  { message: 'At least one field must be provided' }
);

// Infer types from schemas
type CreateUserInput = z.infer<typeof createUserSchema>;
type UserIdParams = z.infer<typeof userIdParams>;
type PaginationQuery = z.infer<typeof paginationQuery>;

// === Type-safe handler helper ===
type TypedHandler<TBody = unknown, TParams = unknown, TQuery = unknown> = (
  req: ValidatedRequest<TBody, TParams, TQuery>,
  res: Response,
  next: NextFunction
) => Promise<void> | void;

function typedHandler<TBody = unknown, TParams = unknown, TQuery = unknown>(
  handler: TypedHandler<TBody, TParams, TQuery>
): RequestHandler {
  return (req, res, next) => {
    const result = handler(req as any, res, next);
    if (result instanceof Promise) {
      result.catch(next);
    }
  };
}

// === Routes with full type safety ===
const app = express();
app.use(express.json());

app.get(
  '/users',
  validate({ query: paginationQuery }),
  typedHandler<unknown, unknown, PaginationQuery>(async (req, res) => {
    const { page, limit, sort, order } = req.validatedQuery;
    // page is number, sort is 'name' | 'created_at' | 'email' — fully typed
    res.json({ page, limit, sort, order, users: [] });
  })
);

app.post(
  '/users',
  validate({ body: createUserSchema }),
  typedHandler<CreateUserInput>(async (req, res) => {
    const { name, email, role } = req.validatedBody;
    // name is string, role is 'user' | 'admin' | 'moderator' — fully typed
    res.status(201).json({ id: 'new-uuid', name, email, role });
  })
);

app.put(
  '/users/:id',
  validate({ params: userIdParams, body: updateUserSchema }),
  typedHandler<Partial<CreateUserInput>, UserIdParams>(async (req, res) => {
    const { id } = req.validatedParams;
    res.json({ id, ...req.validatedBody });
  })
);

app.listen(3000);
```

instruction: How do I implement rate limiting middleware in Express/TypeScript with sliding window, per-user limits, and Redis-backed distributed state?
output:
<think>
Rate limiting in production Express apps needs: 1) A sliding window algorithm (more accurate than fixed windows — no burst at window boundaries), 2) Per-user/per-IP limits with different tiers, 3) Distributed state via Redis (required for multi-instance deployments), 4) Proper headers (RateLimit-Limit, RateLimit-Remaining, RateLimit-Reset per RFC 6585). The sliding window log algorithm stores timestamps of each request and counts how many fall within the window. More memory-efficient: sliding window counter combines two fixed windows with a weighted average. For Redis, we use sorted sets (ZRANGEBYSCORE) for the log approach or two counters for the counter approach. I'll implement the sorted set approach as it's precise and Redis handles the heavy lifting. Key decisions: identify users by API key first, fall back to IP, allow different rate limit tiers (free vs paid), include retry-after headers. The middleware should be configurable per-route. I need to handle Redis failures gracefully — fail open (allow request) rather than blocking users when Redis is down.
</think>

Here's a production rate limiter for Express with Redis:

```typescript
import express, { Request, Response, NextFunction } from 'express';
import Redis from 'ioredis';

// === Rate limit configuration ===
interface RateLimitConfig {
  windowMs: number;      // Window size in milliseconds
  maxRequests: number;   // Max requests per window
  keyPrefix?: string;
  keyGenerator?: (req: Request) => string;
  skipIf?: (req: Request) => boolean;
  onLimitReached?: (req: Request) => void;
}

interface RateLimitTier {
  name: string;
  windowMs: number;
  maxRequests: number;
}

const TIERS: Record<string, RateLimitTier> = {
  free:       { name: 'free',       windowMs: 60_000, maxRequests: 30 },
  basic:     { name: 'basic',      windowMs: 60_000, maxRequests: 100 },
  premium:   { name: 'premium',    windowMs: 60_000, maxRequests: 500 },
  unlimited: { name: 'unlimited',  windowMs: 60_000, maxRequests: Infinity },
};

// === Sliding window rate limiter with Redis sorted sets ===
class SlidingWindowRateLimiter {
  private redis: Redis;

  constructor(redisUrl: string) {
    this.redis = new Redis(redisUrl, {
      maxRetriesPerRequest: 1,
      lazyConnect: true,
      enableReadyCheck: false,
    });

    this.redis.on('error', (err) => {
      console.error('Redis rate limiter error:', err.message);
    });
  }

  async check(
    key: string,
    windowMs: number,
    maxRequests: number
  ): Promise<{ allowed: boolean; remaining: number; resetMs: number }> {
    const now = Date.now();
    const windowStart = now - windowMs;

    try {
      // Atomic sliding window using Redis sorted set + pipeline
      const pipeline = this.redis.pipeline();
      pipeline.zremrangebyscore(key, 0, windowStart);  // Remove expired
      pipeline.zadd(key, now.toString(), `${now}-${Math.random()}`);  // Add current
      pipeline.zcard(key);  // Count in window
      pipeline.pexpire(key, windowMs);  // Set TTL

      const results = await pipeline.exec();
      const count = (results?.[2]?.[1] as number) ?? 0;
      const remaining = Math.max(0, maxRequests - count);
      const allowed = count <= maxRequests;

      if (!allowed) {
        // Remove the request we just added (it was denied)
        await this.redis.zremrangebyscore(key, now, now);
      }

      return {
        allowed,
        remaining,
        resetMs: windowMs - (now - windowStart),
      };
    } catch (err) {
      // Fail open: if Redis is down, allow the request
      console.error('Rate limit check failed, allowing request:', err);
      return { allowed: true, remaining: maxRequests, resetMs: windowMs };
    }
  }

  async close(): Promise<void> {
    await this.redis.quit();
  }
}

// === Middleware factory ===
function rateLimitMiddleware(
  limiter: SlidingWindowRateLimiter,
  config: RateLimitConfig
) {
  const {
    windowMs,
    maxRequests,
    keyPrefix = 'rl',
    keyGenerator = (req) => req.ip || 'unknown',
    skipIf,
    onLimitReached,
  } = config;

  return async (req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction): Promise<void> => {
    if (skipIf?.(req)) {
      next();
      return;
    }

    const identifier = keyGenerator(req);
    const key = `${keyPrefix}:${identifier}`;

    const { allowed, remaining, resetMs } = await limiter.check(
      key, windowMs, maxRequests
    );

    // Set standard rate limit headers
    res.set({
      'RateLimit-Limit': maxRequests.toString(),
      'RateLimit-Remaining': remaining.toString(),
      'RateLimit-Reset': Math.ceil(resetMs / 1000).toString(),
    });

    if (!allowed) {
      onLimitReached?.(req);
      res.status(429).json({
        error: {
          code: 'RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED',
          message: 'Too many requests',
          retryAfter: Math.ceil(resetMs / 1000),
        },
      });
      return;
    }

    next();
  };
}

// === Per-user tier-aware rate limiting ===
function tieredRateLimit(limiter: SlidingWindowRateLimiter) {
  return async (req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction): Promise<void> => {
    const apiKey = req.headers['x-api-key'] as string | undefined;
    const tier = apiKey ? getUserTier(apiKey) : TIERS.free;

    if (tier.maxRequests === Infinity) {
      next();
      return;
    }

    const identifier = apiKey || req.ip || 'unknown';
    const key = `rl:tiered:${identifier}`;

    const { allowed, remaining, resetMs } = await limiter.check(
      key, tier.windowMs, tier.maxRequests
    );

    res.set({
      'RateLimit-Limit': tier.maxRequests.toString(),
      'RateLimit-Remaining': remaining.toString(),
      'RateLimit-Reset': Math.ceil(resetMs / 1000).toString(),
      'X-RateLimit-Tier': tier.name,
    });

    if (!allowed) {
      res.status(429).json({
        error: { code: 'RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED', retryAfter: Math.ceil(resetMs / 1000), tier: tier.name },
      });
      return;
    }
    next();
  };
}

function getUserTier(apiKey: string): RateLimitTier {
  // In production: look up from DB/cache
  if (apiKey.startsWith('premium_')) return TIERS.premium;
  if (apiKey.startsWith('basic_')) return TIERS.basic;
  return TIERS.free;
}

// === App setup ===
const app = express();
const limiter = new SlidingWindowRateLimiter('redis://localhost:6379');

// Global rate limit: 100 req/min per IP
app.use(rateLimitMiddleware(limiter, { windowMs: 60_000, maxRequests: 100 }));

// Per-user tiered limits on API routes
app.use('/api', tieredRateLimit(limiter));

// Strict limit on auth endpoints
app.use('/auth', rateLimitMiddleware(limiter, {
  windowMs: 900_000, maxRequests: 5,
  keyPrefix: 'rl:auth',
}));

app.get('/api/data', (req, res) => res.json({ data: 'hello' }));
app.listen(3000);
```

instruction: How do I implement graceful shutdown in an Express/TypeScript application that handles in-flight requests, WebSocket connections, database pools, and background job queues?
output:
<think>
Graceful shutdown in Node.js/Express requires careful orchestration because: 1) `server.close()` stops new connections but doesn't kill existing ones, 2) Keep-alive connections stay open indefinitely without explicit tracking, 3) WebSocket connections need separate handling, 4) Background jobs (Bull/BullMQ) need to finish or be re-queued, 5) Database connection pools need draining. The shutdown sequence should be: signal received -> stop accepting new requests -> mark health as unhealthy (for load balancer) -> wait for in-flight requests with timeout -> close WebSockets -> drain job queues -> close DB pools -> exit. For tracking active connections, we need to instrument the server's 'connection' event. For a timeout, if requests don't finish within N seconds, force-close. Important Node.js gotcha: `process.on('SIGTERM')` must handle async cleanup properly — you can't just call `process.exit()` in the handler. Also need to handle both SIGTERM (container orchestrator) and SIGINT (Ctrl+C). I should show the connection tracking pattern and timeout-based forced shutdown.
</think>

Here's a production graceful shutdown system for Express/TypeScript:

```typescript
import express, { Request, Response } from 'express';
import http from 'http';
import { Server as WebSocketServer, WebSocket } from 'ws';
import { EventEmitter } from 'events';

// === Connection tracker ===
class ConnectionTracker {
  private connections = new Set<import('net').Socket>();
  private activeRequests = 0;

  track(server: http.Server): void {
    server.on('connection', (socket) => {
      this.connections.add(socket);
      socket.on('close', () => this.connections.delete(socket));
    });
  }

  requestStarted(): void { this.activeRequests++; }
  requestFinished(): void { this.activeRequests--; }

  get stats() {
    return {
      connections: this.connections.size,
      activeRequests: this.activeRequests,
    };
  }

  destroyIdle(): void {
    for (const socket of this.connections) {
      // Destroy sockets with no active requests
      socket.end();
    }
  }

  destroyAll(): void {
    for (const socket of this.connections) {
      socket.destroy();
    }
  }
}

// === Resource manager ===
interface ManagedResource {
  name: string;
  close(): Promise<void>;
}

class DatabasePool implements ManagedResource {
  name = 'database';
  async close(): Promise<void> {
    console.log('Closing database pool...');
    // await pool.end();
  }
}

class RedisClient implements ManagedResource {
  name = 'redis';
  async close(): Promise<void> {
    console.log('Closing Redis connection...');
    // await redis.quit();
  }
}

class JobQueue implements ManagedResource {
  name = 'job-queue';
  async close(): Promise<void> {
    console.log('Draining job queue...');
    // await queue.close(); // BullMQ waits for active jobs
  }
}

// === Graceful shutdown orchestrator ===
class GracefulShutdown extends EventEmitter {
  private isShuttingDown = false;
  private httpServer: http.Server;
  private wsServer: WebSocketServer;
  private tracker: ConnectionTracker;
  private resources: ManagedResource[];
  private shutdownTimeout: number;

  constructor(config: {
    httpServer: http.Server;
    wsServer: WebSocketServer;
    tracker: ConnectionTracker;
    resources: ManagedResource[];
    shutdownTimeoutMs?: number;
  }) {
    super();
    this.httpServer = config.httpServer;
    this.wsServer = config.wsServer;
    this.tracker = config.tracker;
    this.resources = config.resources;
    this.shutdownTimeout = config.shutdownTimeoutMs ?? 30_000;
  }

  get shuttingDown(): boolean {
    return this.isShuttingDown;
  }

  register(): void {
    const signals: NodeJS.Signals[] = ['SIGTERM', 'SIGINT'];
    for (const signal of signals) {
      process.on(signal, () => {
        console.log(`Received ${signal}`);
        this.shutdown().then(() => process.exit(0)).catch(() => process.exit(1));
      });
    }

    process.on('uncaughtException', (err) => {
      console.error('Uncaught exception:', err);
      this.shutdown().then(() => process.exit(1));
    });
  }

  async shutdown(): Promise<void> {
    if (this.isShuttingDown) return;
    this.isShuttingDown = true;
    this.emit('shutdown:start');
    console.log('Starting graceful shutdown...');

    // Force exit after timeout
    const forceTimer = setTimeout(() => {
      console.error('Shutdown timeout — forcing exit');
      this.tracker.destroyAll();
      process.exit(1);
    }, this.shutdownTimeout);
    forceTimer.unref();

    try {
      // 1. Stop accepting new connections
      await new Promise<void>((resolve) => {
        this.httpServer.close(() => resolve());
      });
      console.log('HTTP server closed to new connections');

      // 2. Close WebSocket connections gracefully
      for (const client of this.wsServer.clients) {
        if (client.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) {
          client.close(1001, 'Server shutting down');
        }
      }
      console.log('WebSocket clients notified');

      // 3. Wait for active requests, then destroy idle connections
      await this.waitForRequests(10_000);
      this.tracker.destroyIdle();

      // 4. Close managed resources in reverse order
      for (const resource of [...this.resources].reverse()) {
        try {
          await resource.close();
          console.log(`Closed: ${resource.name}`);
        } catch (err) {
          console.error(`Error closing ${resource.name}:`, err);
        }
      }

      clearTimeout(forceTimer);
      console.log('Graceful shutdown complete');
      this.emit('shutdown:complete');
    } catch (err) {
      console.error('Shutdown error:', err);
      clearTimeout(forceTimer);
    }
  }

  private waitForRequests(timeoutMs: number): Promise<void> {
    return new Promise((resolve) => {
      const check = () => {
        if (this.tracker.stats.activeRequests === 0) {
          resolve();
          return;
        }
        setTimeout(check, 100);
      };
      setTimeout(resolve, timeoutMs); // Don't wait forever
      check();
    });
  }
}

// === Application setup ===
const app = express();
const tracker = new ConnectionTracker();

// Track active requests
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  tracker.requestStarted();
  res.on('finish', () => tracker.requestFinished());
  next();
});

let shutdown: GracefulShutdown;

app.get('/health', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
  if (shutdown?.shuttingDown) {
    res.status(503).json({ status: 'shutting_down' });
    return;
  }
  res.json({ status: 'healthy', ...tracker.stats });
});

app.get('/api/data', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
  res.json({ data: 'hello' });
});

const server = http.createServer(app);
const wss = new WebSocketServer({ server });
tracker.track(server);

const db = new DatabasePool();
const redis = new RedisClient();
const jobs = new JobQueue();

shutdown = new GracefulShutdown({
  httpServer: server,
  wsServer: wss,
  tracker,
  resources: [db, redis, jobs],
  shutdownTimeoutMs: 30_000,
});

shutdown.register();
server.listen(3000, () => console.log('Server running on :3000'));
```
